OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","persona":{"age":41,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"mechanic","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"bachelor's degree","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":3,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["cinema","birdwatching","classical music"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":88,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":17,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":41,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":89,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"As a mechanic with a part-time job and three children, I understand the importance of staying informed about healthcare and COVID vaccines, but I also know that these discussions can be anxiety-inducing. My low openness and high neuroticism make me cautious about engaging in conversations about topics that are complex and emotionally charged. I prefer to stick to what I know and trust, which is why I rely on reputable sources like the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the World Health Organization (WHO) for information. I believe that it's essential to approach these discussions with a sense of humility and a willingness to listen. I don't have all the answers, and neither does anyone else. It's okay to say, 'I don't know' or 'I need more information' before forming an opinion. I also think it's crucial to be respectful of other people's perspectives, even if they differ from our own. We all have different experiences and backgrounds that shape our views, and it's essential to acknowledge that. When discussing healthcare and COVID vaccines, I try to focus on the facts and avoid getting drawn into emotional debates. I ask myself, 'What is the evidence?' and 'What are the experts saying?' before forming an opinion. I also try to be mindful of my anxiety and take breaks when I feel overwhelmed. It's okay to step away from a conversation and come back to it later when I'm feeling more grounded. As a parent, I also think about the impact of these discussions on my children. I want to model healthy communication and critical thinking skills for them. I encourage them to ask questions and seek out reliable sources of information. I also make sure to validate their feelings and create a safe space for them to express their concerns. In conclusion, managing and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines can be challenging, but it's essential to approach them with a sense of humility, respect, and a willingness to listen. By focusing on the facts, being mindful of our anxiety, and modeling healthy communication for our children, we can navigate these conversations with greater ease and confidence.","reasoning_summary":"The persona's low openness and high neuroticism make them cautious about engaging in complex and emotionally charged discussions. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion: As a mechanic with a part-time job and three children, I understand the importance of staying informed about healthcare and COVID vaccines, but I also know that these discussions can be anxiety-inducing. My low openness and high neuroticism make me cautious about engaging in conversations about topics that are complex and emotionally charged. I prefer to stick to what I know and trust, which is why I rely on reputable sources like the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the World Health Organization (WHO) for information. I believe that it's essential to approach these discussions with a sense of humility and a willingness to listen. I don't have all the answers, and neither does anyone else. It's okay to say, 'I don't know' or 'I need more information' before forming an opinion. I also think it's crucial to be respectful of other people's perspectives, even if they differ from our own. We all have different experiences and backgrounds that shape our views, and it's essential to acknowledge that. When discussing healthcare and COVID vaccines, I try to focus on the facts and avoid getting drawn into emotional debates. I ask myself, 'What is the evidence?' and 'What are the experts saying?' before forming an opinion. I also try to be mindful of my anxiety and take breaks when I feel overwhelmed. It's okay to step away from a conversation and come back to it later when I'm feeling more grounded. As a parent, I also think about the impact of these discussions on my children. I want to model healthy communication and critical thinking skills for them. I encourage them to ask questions and seek out reliable sources of information. I also make sure to validate their feelings and create a safe space for them to express their concerns. In conclusion, managing and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines can be challenging, but it's essential to approach them with a sense of humility, respect, and a willingness to listen. By focusing on the facts, being mindful of our anxiety, and modeling healthy communication for our children, we can navigate these conversations with greater ease and confidence.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona's low openness and high neuroticism make them cautious about engaging in complex and emotionally charged discussions. They prefer to rely on reputable sources for information and approach conversations with humility and respect. The persona values facts and evidence and tries to avoid emotional debates. They also consider the impact of these discussions on their children and strive to model healthy communication and critical thinking skills. The persona's anxiety is acknowledged, and they take breaks when feeling overwhelmed.
citations: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
